_comment: English translations for showcase-content.json. groups match by array index (title/intro only); apps match by slug. build-portfolio-site.py embeds both languages and a KO/EN toggle.

groups.title: ⌨️ Productivity · Tools
groups.intro: Practical tools that cut repetitive typing and time-management overhead

groups.title: 🎨 Creativity · Making
groups.intro: Creator tools for making photos, sounds, and drawings with your own hands

groups.title: 🌱 Habits · Wellbeing
groups.intro: Self-care apps for tracking, adjusting, and looking after yourself

groups.title: 💛 Mind · Motivation
groups.intro: Apps that add encouragement and direction to a tiring day

groups.title: 📚 Learning
groups.intro: Learning apps for practicing as much as you want

groups.title: 🗺 Life · Info
groups.intro: Everyday apps that help with daily decisions and planning

groups.title: 🤝 Relationships · Mentoring
groups.intro: Apps for staying on top of the relationships that matter

groups.title: 🛠 Developer Tools
groups.intro: Tools for people who build and run apps

clip-keyboard.tagline: One tap for repeated phrases — and the time saved, in numbers
clip-keyboard.problem: You retype the same messages every day — booking confirmations, bank details — losing time and risking typos.
clip-keyboard.stakeholders: Shop owners, support agents, and office workers who handle frequent customer messages and email
clip-keyboard.persona: Jihyun (34), a workshop owner who sends dozens of identical replies to booking inquiries every day
clip-keyboard.context: During the lunch rush, retyping the same instructions makes her miss other orders.
clip-keyboard.solution: Save your go-to phrases as keyboard buttons, send them with one tap, and watch the time you've saved add up in stats.
clip-keyboard.hook: If you type the same phrases over and over every day
clip-keyboard.highlight: 'Time saved' stats and achievement milestones that grow as you use it

tap-clip-keyboard.tagline: Your iOS text snippets, right on your Mac
tap-clip-keyboard.problem: Snippets saved on your iPhone can't be used on the Mac where you work most, so you end up retyping them.
tap-clip-keyboard.stakeholders: Knowledge workers who move between iPhone and Mac for documents and email
tap-clip-keyboard.persona: Junho (38), a remote marketer who drafts on his phone and finishes on his Mac
tap-clip-keyboard.context: Writing a proposal email on his Mac, he needs a standard phrase that only lives on his phone — and loses his flow.
tap-clip-keyboard.solution: Pull up phrases saved in Clip Keyboard (iOS) right on your Mac and paste them as-is — no more device boundary.
tap-clip-keyboard.hook: If you want your iPhone snippets on your Mac too

double-reminder.tagline: Two alerts, so you never miss it
double-reminder.problem: A single notification is easy to miss, so you keep blowing past the end of talks and meetings.
double-reminder.stakeholders: Presenters, meeting facilitators, trainers — anyone who has to budget time
double-reminder.persona: Sumin (29), a conference presenter who always runs over in 5-minute talks
double-reminder.context: Not knowing it's 3 minutes to the end, she has to cut her conclusion short.
double-reminder.solution: A heads-up alert before the end and a final alert at the end — two reminders so the important moment never slips by.
double-reminder.hook: If you keep missing the moment to wrap up talks and meetings
double-reminder.highlight: Rings twice — before and at the end — so you can be sure

sticky-presenter.tagline: A presentation timer that stays on top of Keynote
sticky-presenter.problem: With no way to check remaining time mid-presentation, your slide pacing falls apart.
sticky-presenter.stakeholders: Speakers, educators, and anyone giving a sales pitch
sticky-presenter.persona: Taeyoung (41), an in-house instructor who finishes 45-minute lectures in 30
sticky-presenter.context: Talking to the audience, he loses track of time and rushes the back half.
sticky-presenter.solution: An always-visible timer floats above Keynote so you can manage remaining time at a glance throughout your talk.
sticky-presenter.hook: If you lose track of time mid-presentation and your pacing collapses

dont-forget.tagline: Your Mac schedule, at a glance on iPhone
dont-forget.problem: Schedules organized on your desktop are a hassle to check on the go, so you miss appointments.
dont-forget.stakeholders: People who manage their schedule on a Mac but are often out and about
dont-forget.persona: Kyunga (33), a sales rep who can't see her office calendar while in the field
dont-forget.context: Out on a client visit, she's unsure of her next meeting time and has to call the office.
dont-forget.solution: Register your events once and see today's schedule instantly in the iPhone app and widget.
dont-forget.hook: If you miss appointments because your Mac calendar isn't with you

schedule-assistant.tagline: An AI assistant that plans your schedule
schedule-assistant.problem: Gathering and juggling events scattered across places drains the energy you needed for actual planning.
schedule-assistant.stakeholders: Busy office workers and freelancers who feel the weight of schedule management
schedule-assistant.persona: Haneul (22), a college student whose week is tangled with classes, part-time work, and study groups
schedule-assistant.context: The calendar is full of events, but with no visible priorities it's hard to know where to start.
schedule-assistant.solution: Connect your calendar and AI consolidates your events, suggests optimal times, and organizes everything without conflicts.
schedule-assistant.hook: If organizing scattered schedules wears you out

think-flow.tagline: A notepad that continues your train of thought
think-flow.problem: Ordinary notes scatter into fragments, making it hard to pick up yesterday's thinking today.
think-flow.stakeholders: People who develop ideas, writing, and plans over long stretches
think-flow.persona: Yeonwoo (27), an aspiring novelist who builds story ideas over several days
think-flow.context: Forgetting the context of a thread from days ago, she starts from scratch every time.
think-flow.solution: A flow-style notepad that appends to your previous thoughts, keeping the thread of your thinking unbroken.
think-flow.hook: If it's hard to continue yesterday's thinking today

punching.tagline: A camera that turns your photos into stickers
punching.problem: Making your own stickers means a separate background-removal app — too much hassle.
punching.stakeholders: Anyone who wants personal, distinctive stickers in their messenger
punching.persona: Bora (31), an office worker who wants stickers of her cat
punching.context: She captures a cute moment but doesn't know how to make it a sticker, so it stays buried in her photo library.
punching.solution: Snap a photo and the background is cut out automatically — instantly a sticker.
punching.hook: If you want stickers made from your own photos
punching.highlight: Snap it, background gone — instant sticker

pixel-mimi.problem: Drawing pixel art usually requires pro graphics tools — a high bar for a hobby.
pixel-mimi.stakeholders: Beginner creators trying pixel art and game sprites for fun
pixel-mimi.persona: Siwoo (19), a student who wants to draw his own indie game characters
pixel-mimi.context: Facing a complex tool, he doesn't even know what to click first.
pixel-mimi.solution: An intuitive tap-the-grid approach anyone can use to finish pixel art.
pixel-mimi.hook: If you want to try pixel art, lightly
pixel-mimi.highlight: A mobile pixel editor you tap with your fingertips

leeocon.problem: Default emoji alone can't fully express the feeling and personality in a conversation.
leeocon.persona: Minseo (21), a college student chatting with friends on iMessage
leeocon.context: Plain text conversations could use something to set the mood.
leeocon.solution: Colorful, charming iMessage sticker packs that add feeling to your chats.
leeocon.hook: If you want to dress up your iMessage chats

sight-singer.tagline: Tap in a score, hear it instantly
sight-singer.problem: Sight-singing practice or checking a melody requires an instrument — you can't just hear it on the spot.
sight-singer.stakeholders: Music learners, beginner composers, choir and vocal students
sight-singer.persona: Jihoon (35), a choir member whose weak sight-reading stalls him at every new score
sight-singer.context: He can't picture the pitches on the page, so practice crawls.
sight-singer.solution: Place notes on a staff and hit play — hear the score immediately.
sight-singer.hook: If you want to hear a score right away
sight-singer.highlight: The notes you tap, played back on the spot

hands-free-metronome.tagline: A metronome you never have to touch
hands-free-metronome.problem: With both hands on your instrument, you can't adjust the metronome — and the tempo work stops.
hands-free-metronome.persona: Jae (24), a guitar student who stops playing every time he wants to change tempo
hands-free-metronome.context: He wants to raise the BPM mid-song but can't take a hand off — the flow breaks.
hands-free-metronome.solution: Hands-free control keeps the beat going even while you play.
hands-free-metronome.hook: If you can't take your hands off to adjust the beat

cooltime.tagline: A habit timer that puts impulses on cooldown
cooltime.problem: When the urge to buy or do something strikes, you act immediately — then the regret follows, again and again.
cooltime.stakeholders: People who want to curb impulse buying and impulsive behavior
cooltime.persona: Hyunsoo (37), an office worker who hits the pay button in the heat of the moment
cooltime.context: "Just this one" — he splurges impulsively, then regrets it moments later.
cooltime.solution: Like a skill cooldown in a game, it puts a waiting period on spending and actions, creating a gap between impulse and execution.
cooltime.hook: If you splurge on impulse and regret it later
cooltime.highlight: A cooldown on your actions, like a game skill

rainbow-of-desire.tagline: See and balance everything you've taken on
rainbow-of-desire.problem: Enthusiasm keeps you starting new things until you're overloaded — and the sense of accomplishment fades.
rainbow-of-desire.stakeholders: People at risk of burnout from multitasking
rainbow-of-desire.persona: Daeun (30), a planner running five side projects at once
rainbow-of-desire.context: Lots of things started, nothing finished — always chased, always tired.
rainbow-of-desire.solution: Recognize and regulate the habit of taking on too much, manage your workload, and recover a sense of growth.
rainbow-of-desire.hook: If you keep taking on more until you're overloaded
rainbow-of-desire.highlight: Notice and rebalance your 'starting too much' habit

rainbow-workshop.problem: With no sense of where the week's hours went, planning and reflection are guesswork.
rainbow-workshop.stakeholders: Office workers and students who want to use time better
rainbow-workshop.persona: Yujin (28), a freelancer who wonders every weekend what she even did this week
rainbow-workshop.context: She knows she was busy, but with nothing to show she can't find where time leaks.
rainbow-workshop.solution: Stack your week into blocks to see exactly where your time went, at a glance.
rainbow-workshop.hook: If you have no idea where your week went

rebound-journal.problem: You just endure hard times as they pass, leaving no trace of what actually helped you get better.
rebound-journal.stakeholders: People going through a rough patch or getting back up after failure
rebound-journal.persona: Seongmin (33), an office worker rebuilding after burnout and setbacks
rebound-journal.context: Emotions swirl, but without processing the recovery, the same struggles shake him again.
rebound-journal.solution: Record your recovery step by step, visualize small wins, and turn them into a foothold for growth.
rebound-journal.hook: If hard times are just passing you by

three-meals.tagline: Better eating habits, starting with one photo
three-meals.problem: You skip or rush meals, yet never see your eating habits objectively.
three-meals.stakeholders: People interested in dieting and health management
three-meals.persona: Areum (26), an office worker who skips lunch when things get busy
three-meals.context: By day's end she can't even remember what she ate.
three-meals.solution: Log every meal with a photo in 10 seconds, get weekly analysis, and build healthier patterns.
three-meals.hook: If you can't remember what you ate today
three-meals.highlight: One photo — AI reads your eating habits

burning-my-life.tagline: Watch your free time burn away
burning-my-life.problem: Evenings and leisure time slip through your fingers, but the loss never registers.
burning-my-life.stakeholders: People who want their free time to mean something
burning-my-life.persona: Donghyuk (32), an office worker who scrolls his phone after work until he falls asleep
burning-my-life.context: He ends each day regretting that he 'did nothing again today.'
burning-my-life.solution: Visualizes your remaining free time as burning away, making the finiteness of time impossible to ignore.
burning-my-life.hook: If you can feel your free time just disappearing

relax-on.tagline: Sleep sounds, mixed to your taste
relax-on.problem: You waste time hunting for sleep sounds on YouTube, and they never adjust the way you want.
relax-on.stakeholders: People who need sound for sleep, rest, or focus
relax-on.persona: Soyeon (29), a sleepless office worker who spends 30 minutes finding a sound before bed
relax-on.context: The sound she finally picks isn't what she hoped, and she tosses and turns fiddling with volume and mix.
relax-on.solution: Pick nature sounds and mix them to your taste — your own custom sleep soundscape.
relax-on.hook: If finding a sleep sound every night is a chore
relax-on.highlight: Nature sounds, mixed exactly to your taste

daily-compliment.problem: Comparison and self-criticism chip away at your self-esteem, with no ritual to lift yourself up.
daily-compliment.stakeholders: Anyone who needs a positive day — especially those with low self-esteem
daily-compliment.persona: Yerin (25), a young professional who gets gloomy scrolling social media
daily-compliment.context: Comparing herself to others on social media, she starts the day putting herself down.
daily-compliment.solution: A personalized compliment every morning opens the day on a positive note and builds a self-esteem habit.
daily-compliment.hook: If comparison is wearing down your self-esteem
daily-compliment.highlight: A personalized compliment, delivered every morning

bucket-climb.problem: Your aspirations stay a vague list — motivation is weak and achievement never feels real.
bucket-climb.stakeholders: 20- and 30-somethings setting and pursuing life goals
bucket-climb.persona: Mingyu (24), who wants to do a lot but keeps putting it off
bucket-climb.context: Bucket lists get written, then forgotten out of sight — and achievements leave no record.
bucket-climb.solution: Register dreams with photos for visual motivation, and turn completed ones into a storybook of memories.
bucket-climb.hook: If your dreams remain vague and distant
bucket-climb.highlight: Captured in photos — and once achieved, a storybook of memories

speak-flow.tagline: Drill the phrases you actually need until they stick
speak-flow.problem: Textbook sentences differ from what you'd actually say, so what you learn never comes out of your mouth.
speak-flow.stakeholders: Learners who need real-world English conversation
speak-flow.persona: Jiwon (31), an office worker preparing for overseas meetings
speak-flow.context: The expressions she actually wants aren't in any textbook, so she mumbles through every time.
speak-flow.solution: Register the conversations you want to practice and drill them on repeat until they're second nature.
speak-flow.hook: If the English you learned won't come out of your mouth

sanoramyeon.problem: Even to learn basic Indonesian, there's no easy, low-pressure way to start.
sanoramyeon.stakeholders: Beginners who need Indonesian for travel or work
sanoramyeon.persona: Nayeon (30), a traveler preparing to spend a month in Bali
sanoramyeon.context: She worries she won't manage even simple greetings and orders on the ground.
sanoramyeon.solution: Learn Indonesian lightly through short, useful expressions and pick up basic conversation.
sanoramyeon.hook: If you want to pick up basic Indonesian, lightly

donkko-mart.tagline: Check if the store is open before you go
donkko-mart.problem: You head to the store only to find it closed — a wasted trip.
donkko-mart.stakeholders: Anyone who shops at offline marts and stores
donkko-mart.persona: Taeyun (34), living alone, who stops for groceries on the way home
donkko-mart.context: Arriving late at night, the store's already closed and he goes home empty-handed.
donkko-mart.solution: Check store holidays and opening hours before you leave — no more wasted trips.
donkko-mart.hook: If you've ever arrived at a closed store and walked home empty-handed

life-restaurant.problem: Great restaurants live only in your memory — later you can't recall the name, let alone recommend it.
life-restaurant.stakeholders: Foodies who love dining out and exploring restaurants
life-restaurant.persona: Juha (28), an office worker who always says 'where was that place again?' when friends ask
life-restaurant.context: She loved the place, but the name is gone — no revisit, no recommendation.
life-restaurant.solution: Collect photos, notes, and your own ratings in one place — a personal best-restaurants map you can recommend from.
life-restaurant.hook: If you can't remember the name of that great restaurant
life-restaurant.highlight: Every place you've loved becomes your own collection on a map

shared-day-designer.tagline: Maximum holidays from minimum leave days
shared-day-designer.problem: Figuring out where to place leave days for the longest break means tedious calendar math.
shared-day-designer.stakeholders: Office workers who want to spend their leave strategically
shared-day-designer.persona: Hyunwoo (33), an associate who wants to stretch bridge holidays as far as they'll go
shared-day-designer.context: Unsure where to slot leave between holidays and weekends, he guesses every time.
shared-day-designer.solution: Combines public holidays, weekends, and your leave to automatically design the longest break from the fewest days.
shared-day-designer.hook: If you want maximum holiday from minimum leave
shared-day-designer.highlight: Auto-designs the longest break from the fewest leave days

fire-tracker.tagline: A calculator for your retirement date
fire-tracker.problem: 'When could I actually quit or retire?' stays nothing more than a vague anxiety.
fire-tracker.stakeholders: Office workers planning early retirement (FIRE) or a career exit
fire-tracker.persona: Seungho (36), a thirty-something office worker dreaming of FIRE
fire-tracker.context: He only has gut feelings about his target amount and date, so the plan keeps wobbling.
fire-tracker.solution: Enter your numbers to calculate the money you need and when you can retire — and set concrete goals.
fire-tracker.hook: If you have no idea when you could retire

korea-navi.problem: Mainstream map apps neglect accessibility, making navigation itself a barrier for some.
korea-navi.stakeholders: All pedestrians, including users who need accessibility support
korea-navi.persona: Hanbyul (40), a visually impaired user who navigates with a screen reader
korea-navi.context: Following directions in a map app with a complex UI is a struggle.
korea-navi.solution: An accessibility-first UI guides everyone to their destination with ease.
korea-navi.hook: If you need directions that respect accessibility

probability-calculator.problem: You pick numbers on gut feeling alone, with no way to check the odds — even just for fun.
probability-calculator.stakeholders: People who enjoy number and probability games
probability-calculator.persona: Kwangsoo (45), a lotto fan who agonizes over his numbers every week
probability-calculator.context: He picks combinations purely on instinct and always wonders if there's a better way.
probability-calculator.solution: Generates new numbers from past draws and calculates probabilities, adding a layer of fun.
probability-calculator.hook: If you're picking numbers on gut feeling alone
probability-calculator.highlight: Probability in graphs, no formulas needed

rapport-map.tagline: A compass that records the context of your relationships
rapport-map.problem: Contacts hold only names — when you met and what you talked about gets lost.
rapport-map.stakeholders: People who maintain many relationships through mentoring, networking, and client care
rapport-map.persona: Jaewon (42), a community mentor looking after 40 mentees
rapport-map.context: Focused on the vocal ones, he unknowingly neglects the quiet mentees.
rapport-map.solution: Record dates, conversations, and promises per person — review the context before each meeting and leave no one behind.
rapport-map.hook: If you have too many people to keep up with
rapport-map.highlight: Beyond contacts — the full context of each relationship

ring-rong.tagline: See where your mentor or contact is
ring-rong.problem: Before a meetup or mentoring session, not knowing where the other person is wastes coordination time.
ring-rong.stakeholders: People coordinating in-person meetups and mentoring
ring-rong.persona: Gaeun (37), a mentor arranging a meeting spot with her mentee
ring-rong.context: Not knowing where each other is, they trade phone calls about location — tedious.
ring-rong.solution: Check the other person's location and connect meetups and mentoring effortlessly.
ring-rong.hook: If figuring out where the other person is before meeting is a hassle

review-manager.tagline: A review command center for indie developers
review-manager.problem: Run several apps and reviews and ratings scatter — you end up triaging by gut feeling.
review-manager.stakeholders: Indie developers and small studios running multiple apps
review-manager.persona: Leeo (35), a solo developer running 10 apps alone
review-manager.context: Hopping between App Store Connect pages per app, he misses a bad review on an important app.
review-manager.solution: Gathers all app reviews in one place and auto-recommends 'the app to watch now' with risk/opportunity scores.
review-manager.hook: If reviews scattered across apps are hard to manage
review-manager.highlight: Risk and opportunity scores point to today's priority app

tetra-tint.tagline: A contrast color tool for developers
tetra-tint.problem: You eyeball whether UI color contrast meets accessibility standards — and it's easy to get wrong.
tetra-tint.stakeholders: Developers and designers responsible for color contrast and accessibility
tetra-tint.persona: Minjae (31), a front-end developer never quite sure about dark-mode contrast
tetra-tint.context: Choosing text and background colors, he's never confident the contrast is sufficient.
tetra-tint.solution: Check contrast-safe colors easily and pick ones that meet accessibility standards.
tetra-tint.hook: If you're unsure your color contrast passes accessibility
tetra-tint.highlight: A dev tool that shows contrast colors at a glance

junior-mistakes.problem: Junior developers hide mistakes out of embarrassment, missing the chance to grow from them.
junior-mistakes.persona: Dohyun (26), a first-year developer who beats himself up over small mistakes
junior-mistakes.context: He repeats the same mistakes but never writes them down, so nothing becomes a lesson.
junior-mistakes.solution: 100 common developer mistakes that turn failure into a stepping stone for growth.
junior-mistakes.hook: If mistakes scare you as a junior developer

sunflower.tagline: Track the sunlight you got today
sunflower.problem: With no sense of how much sun you've gotten, indoor life quietly leaves you short on daylight.
sunflower.stakeholders: People who spend long hours indoors and risk running low on sunlight and vitamin D
sunflower.persona: Jia (29), an office worker who never sees the sun during the workday
sunflower.context: Only when her body starts feeling heavy does she realize she hasn't seen the sun in days.
sunflower.solution: Log today's sunlight and build a habit of getting regular time in the sun.
sunflower.hook: If you have no idea how much sun you're getting

slide-snap.tagline: Capture and organize presentation slides with ease
slide-snap.problem: Slides shot in a hurry come out crooked and scatter across your photo library, impossible to find later.
slide-snap.stakeholders: Audiences who capture slides at lectures, seminars, and classes
slide-snap.persona: Yuna (26), a grad student who snaps seminar slides indiscriminately
slide-snap.context: Slides shot during talks get mixed into her photo library and are hard to find again.
slide-snap.solution: Capture and organize slides comfortably as you listen, and find them instantly later.
slide-snap.hook: If your hastily shot slides end up in chaos

kid-calendar.tagline: Snap paper notices, manage them digitally
kid-calendar.problem: Kids' school and academy schedules arrive on paper — copying them by hand, things slip through.
kid-calendar.stakeholders: Parents managing children's paper schedules and notices
kid-calendar.persona: Eunjung (38), a working mom managing two kids' schedules from paper handouts
kid-calendar.context: She forgets to copy a notice over and the school supplies don't get packed.
kid-calendar.solution: Photograph paper schedules and manage them all in one digital place.
kid-calendar.hook: If paper notices from your kids keep slipping through

flip-book.tagline: Kids' drawings come alive as animation
flip-book.problem: A child's drawing stays frozen on paper — there's no easy path to the delight of seeing it move.
flip-book.stakeholders: Parents who want to encourage their child's drawing and creativity
flip-book.persona: Sujin (35), a parent who often draws together with her child
flip-book.context: Her child draws but quickly loses interest and doesn't keep going.
flip-book.solution: Kids draw easily and watch their drawings come to life, growing the joy of creating.
flip-book.hook: If you wish your child's drawings could move
